content.assessment: A regional pilot is well-suited to network-planning evaluation since it can be scoped to specific geographic areas with known capacity and transport profiles, but representativeness of volunteer regions and the acknowledged need to pair de-tracking with zoning controls (as in T4) raise network-generalizability concerns, compounded by centralized constraints on local adaptation.
opportunities: Bounded pilot scale allows testing of transport and capacity feasibility questions directly relevant to this lens before committing network-wide resources
strengths: Piloting in bounded regions allows network-capacity and transport feasibility to be assessed empirically before national rollout
Explicit pairing with zoning/choice controls (linking to T4) acknowledges that de-tracking alone is insufficient against network-level segregation drivers
threats: Rural teacher shortages could differentially affect pilot regions depending on selection, skewing results away from network-representative conditions
Even a successful pilot may fail to translate into durable national network reform, as seen in comparable reversal precedents elsewhere
weaknesses: Centralized KLIK control over maintenance and planning may limit a pilot region's ability to adapt zoning or staffing to local network conditions
Volunteering pilot regions may not represent the geographic diversity of settlement types, transport access levels, or teacher-capacity conditions found nationally
